EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed in the vicinity of Donnelly Island and Donnelly Brook...on the Aroostook River...during the early morning hours of the 10th. Minor flooding developed upriver from the jam which impacted Gardner Creek Road. Another ice jam developed near Chruchill and Bull Islands...in the vicinity of Crouseville...that afternoon. Flooding from this ice jam impacted Washburn Road with minor flooding. The ice jams released during the early afternoon of the 11th allowing river levels to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several ice jams impacted portions of the Aroostook River from the vicinity of Washburn to the vicinity of Crouseville. The ice jams developed during the early morning hours of the 10th and persisted into the afternoon of the 11th. Flooding from these ice jams mostly impacted portions of Gardner Creek Road and Washburn Road. The flooding was generally minor. The ice jams released during the afternoon of the 11th allowing river levels to fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture was drawn north across the region from the 14th into the 15th in advance of a slow moving cold front. Areas of heavy rain lifted north in advance of the front...enhanced by several lows lifting north along the slow moving front. The heaviest rains occurred from the overnight hours of the 14th into the morning of the 15th. Flash flooding developed in both the Bangor/Brewer and Lincoln/Mattawamkeag areas during the early morning hours of the 15th. Numerous roads were closed and impassable due to flash flooding...with vehicles also disabled. Water rescues were also required around Bangor/Brewer with up to 4 feet of standing water reported at some locations. Several roads were also closed in the Lincoln/Mattawamkeag area including Route 155 and Old Military Road. Old Military Road was partially collapsed due to rushing water overwhelming a culvert. Other roads and culverts were also damaged to varying extents across southern and central Penobscot county. Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed during the early morning hours of the 15th around the Bangor/Brewer area. Numerous roads were closed and impassable due to the flash flooding. Some water rescues were also required...with standing water up to 4 feet deep in some locations. Several vehicles were also disabled due to the flash flood waters. Some roads and culverts in the area were damaged to varying extents. Rainfall totals with this event generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.50 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeaster storm brought widespread gusty winds to Maine that downed branches and a few trees causing power outages to the region.  Winds remained below high wind warning criteria but gusts mostly in the 40 to 50 mph range along with heavy rain still downed numerous branches and trees causing scattered power outages across Coastal and Western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty southeast winds through the day on October 14th along with heavy rain caused numerous snapped branches and a few downed standing dead trees.  Power outages in Southwest Maine peaked at 69,500 outages during the afternoon hours when the peak wind gusts were occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large synoptic storm system was bringing gusty winds to the region with wind gusts in the 40 mph range. A convective squall line developed across southwest New Hampshire within the larger storm and tracked into Southern York County before weakening. The thunderstorm combined with the strong synoptic winds to bring damaging winds to Kittery and South Berwick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective outflow winds associated with a severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in South Berwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked through the Gulf of Maine during the 29th...exiting across the Maritimes during the 30th. Snow expanded north across the region through the morning into the early afternoon of the 29th. Snow...heavy at times with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour...then persisted through the afternoon into the early morning hours of the 30th. The snow then diminished through the remainder of the morning of the 30th...though areas of blowing snow persisted. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph...occurred across much of the region. Gusts up to around 65 mph occurred along the Downeast coast. Extensive blowing and drifting snow with white-outs and near zero visibilities occurred. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon and evening of the 29th. Storm total snow accumulations Downeast generally ranged from 10 to 18 inches...with local totals in excess of 20 inches. Snow totals across much of the rest of the region generally ranged from 10 to 16 inches...decreasing to 5 to 10 inches across extreme northern and northwest areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 13 inches. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 50 mph...produced near blizzard conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 16th low pressure formed over the Gulf Coast and began to lift northeast. The low continued to track along the East Coast and strengthen until eventually occluding near Chesapeake Bay late in the evening on the 16th. The low continued deepening into central New York where it ultimately got picked up by the next trough and moved northeast. Ahead of the warm front a strong low level jet developed and brought a round of moderate to heavy precipitation to western Maine on the 17th. The strongest forcing moved so quickly that mixed precipitation across the interior did not last for a significant amount of time before coming to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection forced a laterally translating band of snow into western Maine early in the morning of the 17th. Within a couple hours snow was moderate to heavy, especially for the interior. Snowfall rates were near 1 inch per hour for two or three hours leading up to mid morning. Closer to the coast mild temperatures resulted in only a brief period of snow. The dry slot and warmer mid level temperatures were only about 6 to 8 hours behind the onset of snow. Snow changed over to or mixed with some sleet and freezing rain before ending early in the afternoon. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 or 3 inches near the Maine Turnpike to as much as a foot near the Saco River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a stalled frontal boundary in the southern Appalachians and began to deepen at is approached the mouth of Chesapeake Bay. Over the next 9 hours the storm strengthened another 16 mb, however it tracked outside of the benchmark and remained on the outskirts of the Gulf of Maine. A laterally translating quasi-stationary band of heavy snowfall developed over southern New England and slid east northeastward through the Boston area but not quite close enough to affect more than the extreme southwestern Maine coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread coastal York County very early in the morning on the 7th. Snow was moderate to heavy through daybreak, after which the snow remained generally light through mid afternoon. The heaviest snow remained just offshore, but snow totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 16th low pressure formed over the Gulf Coast and began to lift northeast. The low continued to track along the East Coast and strengthen until eventually occluding near Chesapeake Bay late in the evening on the 16th. The low continued deepening into central New York where it ultimately got picked up by the next trough and moved northeast. Ahead of the warm front a strong low level jet developed and brought a round of strong wind gusts to coastal western Maine through the day on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds began to ramp up after daybreak on the 17th. Wind gusts sharply increased around 8 AM and continued until about 10 AM before gradually tapering off through the afternoon. The strongest wind gusts were in the 60 to 65 mph range, especially close to the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 16th low pressure formed over the Gulf Coast and began to lift northeast. The low continued to track along the East Coast and strengthen until eventually occluding near Chesapeake Bay late in the evening on the 16th. The low continued deepening into central New York where it ultimately got picked up by the next trough and moved northeast. Ahead of the warm front a strong low level jet developed and brought a round of strong wind gusts to coastal western Maine through the day on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds began to ramp up after daybreak on the 17th. Wind gusts sharply increased around 8 AM and continued until about 10 AM before gradually tapering off through the afternoon. The Portland Jetport gusted to 54 mph at 912 AM. The strongest wind gusts were in the 60 to 65 mph range, especially close to the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 16th low pressure formed over the Gulf Coast and began to lift northeast. The low continued to track along the East Coast and strengthen until eventually occluding near Chesapeake Bay late in the evening on the 16th. The low continued deepening into central New York where it ultimately got picked up by the next trough and moved northeast. Ahead of the warm front a strong low level jet developed and brought a round of moderate to heavy precipitation to western Maine on the 17th. The strongest forcing moved so quickly that mixed precipitation across the interior did not last for a significant amount of time before coming to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection forced a laterally translating band of snow into the western Maine mountains during the morning of the 17th. Within a couple hours snow was moderate to heavy. Snowfall rates were near 1 inch per hour for two or three hours leading up to midday. The dry slot and warmer mid level temperatures were only about 6 to 8 hours behind the onset of snow. Snow briefly mixed with some sleet and freezing rain before ending in the afternoon. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified into a strong storm (980 mb) and tracked over western New England on the morning of January 17th. This allowed for a strong easterly flow to develop as the system passed to our west. Winds peaked around the time of high tide with gusts over hurricane force reported across the Gulf of Maine. Astronomical tides were marginal during the event, however very high storm surge values tied for the eleventh highest of all-time during the midday high tide cycle on the 17th. The surge and large waves caused minor flooding across York and Cumberland counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 9.6 feet combined with a storm surge of 3.1 feet at high tide to produce a 12.7 foot (MLLW) storm tide during the late morning hours. Flood stage in Portland is 12.0 feet (MLLW).  The tide remained above flood stage for a full three hours and 26 minutes at the Portland tide gage.  In Portland, the intersection of Somerset and Pearl streets flooded up to two feet deep near the Whole Foods grocery store.  Flooding was also reported along the Portland Pier with over a foot of inundation in the normal vulnerable low-lying areas.  The intense easterly winds and high storm tides led to closed roads due to large battering surf and water flooding inland tidal areas.  In Scarborough, route one was closed in both the north and south lanes due to flooding from the marsh.  Parking lots and roads were reported flooded in Cape Elizabeth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two heavy rain events in 4 days led to localized flooding in southern Piscataquis county. The first heavy rain event on the 14th and 15th produced 2.50 to 4.50 inches across southern Piscataquis county. This event left the ground saturated and water levels elevated. A second heavy rain event occurred from the 18th into the 19th. Abundant moisture was again drawn north in advance of a slow moving occluded front. Heavy rain developed during the overnight of the 18th into the morning of the 19th. Rainfall totals across southern Piscataquis county generally ranged from 2.00 to 3.00 inches from the 18th into the 19th...orographically enhanced due to upslope into the higher terrain. A railroad bed was undercut and compromised due to the failure of a culvert which was overwhelmed due to localized flooding. This led to the collapse of the rail bed which left the tracks unsupported. A train derailed during the early morning hours of the 19th while crossing the unsupported tracks. The first seven cars of the train derailed. The derailment occurred near Orneville. No injuries were reported. Removal of the derailed cars took nearly a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second heavy rain event in 4 days led to flooding which contributed to a train derailment. The first heavy rain event from the 14th into the 15th produced general 2.50 to 4.50 inch rain totals. This left the ground saturated and water levels elevated. A second heavy rain event from the 18th into the 19th produced an additional 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain. Flooding developed during the early morning hours of the 19th. The flooding overwhelmed a culvert located beneath a rail bed. Failure of the culvert led to a washout and collapse of the rail bed leaving the tracks unsupported. The unsupported tracks contributed to a train derailment during the early morning hours of the 19th. The first seven cars of a train derailed while crossing the unsupported tracks. No injuries occurred. The time of the initial washout is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad southwest flow and a mild February air mass was in place across the region on the 3rd. As a very slow moving cold front approached New England precipitation overspread the area. Given how warm temperatures were, precipitation started as rain. As high pressure built into Quebec through midnight the northern end of the cold front accelerated southward through New England. Rain changed to snow from north to south. A weak wave of low pressure formed and rode northeast along the boundary. This enhanced precipitation during the morning of the 4th. Precipitation ended as snow for all areas during the evening of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 10:00 pm on the 3rd. During the early morning on the 4th the snow intensity increased and became moderate to heavy at times through mid morning. Steady snow continued through the day and tapered off in the evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 13 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 3:00 am on the 4th. Around daybreak on the 4th the snow intensity increased and became moderate at times farther away from the coast through mid-morning. Closer to the immediate coast remained mostly a mix before ending as light snowfall limiting accumulations. Steady light snow continued through the day and tapered off in the evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 inches near Route 202 to 7 inches near Cornish.

Steady light snow continued through the day and tapered off in the evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 inches near Pownal to 8 inches near Sebago Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed on the 24th along a warm front draped across the Southeast. The low deepened gradually as it lifted northeast through the Tennessee and then Ohio River Valleys during the evening of the 24th. An area of high pressure branched to the north of the advancing system, with cold air damming spilling down the eastern slopes of the Appalachians. The primary low pressure began to fill near Lake Erie with secondary low pressure developing off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This low became the dominant one near Nantucket by midday on the 25th. A band of snow lifted into northern New England and then slid east through the day on the 25th, as the center of low pressure raced east northeast to the south of Nova Scotia.  Heavy snow fell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the western lakes and mountains early in the morning on the 25th. A hybrid type snow band pushed through the area then gradually transitioned into a laterally quasi-stationary band which slid back through the area around midday. Moderate to heavy snowfall resulted from the hybrid band, while snow became moderate again as the quasi-stationary band slid out to sea. The snow tapered off in the early evening as the storm headed into the northwest Atlantic. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

Moderate to heavy snowfall resulted from the hybrid band, with moderate snow continuing into the afternoon enhanced by the coastal front and subtle upslope flow into the Camden Hills. The snow tapered off in the early evening as the storm headed into the northwest Atlantic. Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the region during the 27th. Heavy snow showers and squalls developed in advance of the front during the afternoon and evening. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph along with  heavy snow accompanied the snow squalls leading to whiteout conditions and hazardous travel at times. The snow squalls produced localized snow accumulations of 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls occurred in advance of a strong cold front crossing the region. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph...brief heavy snow and whiteout conditions accompanied the snow squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Little Black River during the afternoon of the 13th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 15th. Water levels rose upriver from the jam. The rising waters led to minor flooding along portions of Walker Brook Road...Rapid Road and Little Black Road. The flooding also impacted several homes and structures along the river. The ice jam released during the early morning hours of the 15th allowing river levels to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Little Black River produced minor flooding along portions of Walker Brook Road...Rapid Road and Little Black Road. The flooding also impacted several homes and structures along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 19th a low pressure north of the Great Lakes tracked through the St. Lawrence River Valley. Aloft a low amplitude shortwave trough went from positively to negatively tilted as it crossed the Northeast, helping to drive a cold front through the region. The temperatures at 850 hPa fell from -10 degrees Celsius to -16 degrees Celsius by the morning of the 20th.  Along the leading edge of arctic air snow showers and snow squalls crossed northern New England in the afternoon and early evening causing dangerous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls crossed the western lakes and mountains between 500 pm and 600 pm. The  Steven A. Bean Municipal Airport in Rangeley observed a visibility of 1/4 mile at 535 pm, and though winds did not gust at the airport they were reported gusty in other locations in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving occluding low remained in the vicinity of the Bay of Fundy from the 28th into the 29th. Precipitation transitioned from rain to snow across Aroostook county during the early morning hours of the 28th with colder air being drawn south. Precipitation then remained in the form of snow into the 29th. The heaviest snow accumulations occurred in an area from Caribou to Mars Hill in northeast Aroostook county. Accumulations in this area generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with an isolated 8 inch total. Northwest of the region of heaviest accumulation...lesser precipitation led to less snow. A mix of rain and snow limited snow accumulations to the south. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph accompanied the snow along with much below normal level temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches. Winds gusts of 25 to 35 mph also occurred along with much below normal level temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad southwest flow and a mild February air mass was in place across the region on the 3rd. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed on the 24th along a warm front draped across the Southeast. The primary low pressure began to fill near Lake Erie with secondary low pressure developing off the Mid-Atlantic coast. This low became the dominant one near Nantucket by midday on the 25th. A band of snow lifted into northern New England and then slid east through the day on the 25th, as the center of low pressure raced east northeast to the south of Nova Scotia.  Heavy snow fell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the western lakes and mountains early in the morning on the 25th. A hybrid type snow band gradually transitioned into a laterally quasi-stationary band around daybreak. Moderate to heavy snowfall continued from that time through early afternoon, aided by upslope flow into the Mahoosucs. The snow tapered off in the early evening as the storm headed into the northwest Atlantic. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front drove through the Northeast on the evening of the 27th. The upper shortwave trough was rapidly transitioning to negatively tilted and at its strongest forcing for ascent as it crossed the local area. The temperatures at 850 hPa dropped from around -10 degrees Celsius in the evening to -19 degrees Celsius by morning. Low level lapse rates were very steep, approaching 9 degrees Celsius per kilometer. This led to some small amounts of low level CAPE, which considering the time of year were quite impressive. The result was a well organized line of snow squalls that affected the majority of the local forecast area, bringing widespread travel impacts due to dangerous short term blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls crossed the western lakes and mountains between 5:00 pm and 6:00 pm. The Steven A. Bean Municipal Airport observed a visibility of less than 1/4 mile and a wind gust of 46 knots at 5:15 pm.  Spotter in Sandy River reported visibility less than 100 feet in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the region during the 27th. Heavy snow showers and squalls developed in advance of the front during the afternoon and evening. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph along with  heavy snow accompanied the snow squalls leading to whiteout conditions and hazardous travel at times. The snow squalls produced localized snow accumulations of 1 to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls occurred in advance of a strong cold front crossing the region. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph...brief heavy snow and whiteout conditions accompanied the snow squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving occluding low remained in the vicinity of the Bay of Fundy from the 28th into the 29th. Precipitation transitioned from rain to snow across Aroostook county during the early morning hours of the 28th with colder air being drawn south. Precipitation then remained in the form of snow into the 29th. The heaviest snow accumulations occurred in an area from Caribou to Mars Hill in northeast Aroostook county. Accumulations in this area generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with an isolated 8 inch total. Northwest of the region of heaviest accumulation...lesser precipitation led to less snow. A mix of rain and snow limited snow accumulations to the south. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph accompanied the snow along with much below normal level temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with a maximum total of 8 inches near Mars Hill. Winds gusts of 25 to 35 mph also occurred along with much below normal level temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked east of the region...toward Nova Scotia...through the night of the 7th and the 8th. A trof extended west of the low back across the region. At the same time...an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Snow developed during the overnight hours of the 7th...then persisted through the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th leading to an extended duration snow event. The heaviest snow accumulations occurred across northeast and east-central portions of the region. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 8th. The heaviest snow accumulations occurred across portions of eastern Aroostook county with 10 to 16 inch totals. Snow totals across northwest Aroostook county ranged from 8 to 12 inches. Snow totals in an area from northern Somerset...to southern Piscataquis...to northern Washington counties generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure tracked east of the Gulf of Maine...toward Nova Scotia...from the 14th into the 15th. Snow developed during the afternoon of 14th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 15th. The western edge of the precipitation shield clipped central and coastal portions of Washington county producing generally 2 to 3 inches of snow. However...the snow was accompanied by strong winds. Winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with reduced visibilities along with hazardous travel. Snow totals rapidly diminished west of Washington county. However...strong gusty winds also occurred across portions of Hancock county. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph...occurred across coastal portions of Hancock county. Wind gusts around 60 mph were reported at both East Surry and Bar Harbor. Only minor snow accumulations occurred across Hancock county.|Arctic air was drawn across the region in the wake of the low...along with gusty winds. Dangerous wind chills occurred across northern Maine from later on the 14th into the 15th. Temperatures fell to 10 to 20 below zero. Winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph...with gusts in excess of 40 mph. The winds and temperatures combined to produce wind chill readings of 40 to 50 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph...with gusts in excess of 40 mph...combined with temperatures of 10 to 20 below zero to produce wind chills of 40 to 50 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed off the Florida coast on the 28th and gradually drifted north into the evening. The storm began to rapidly strengthen as the upper trough started to take on a negative tilt very early on the 29th. The storm tracked north northeast to a position off Cape Cod where it briefly stalled before continuing on towards Nova Scotia early on the 30th. The storm remained too far to the east to bring much warm air advection precipitation to the area, but strong frontogenesis occurred in the mid levels northwest of the low center. The result was a heavy band of snowfall that more or less paralleled the coast before pivoting inland over central and Downeast Maine. In addition to the heavy snowfall, a strong northeast low level jet developed and brought damaging wind gusts to much of the coastline. Blizzard conditions occurred at the coast and towards I-95 in western Maine and near-blizzard conditions occurred well inland from there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began on the morning of the 29th, with the intensity of snowfall picking up after daybreak. An intense band of snow developed around mid morning over southern New England but remained mostly over the Gulf of Maine until midday. The band backed into coastal and Midcoast Maine and remained relatively stationary until into the evening. Some moderate to occasionally heavy snowfall did occur west of the band before snow tapered off. Snowfall ranged from 6 inches near Coburn Gore to 12 inches near Saddleback Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked west of Maine during the 17th drawing an occluded front across the region. A strong low level jet moved along the Downeast coast in advance of the occlusion through the morning and early afternoon. East to southeast winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts in excess of 60 mph. In Hancock county...a 65 mph wind gust was reported at Naskeag...with a 60 mph wind gust at Sedgwick Ridge. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were also reported across coastal Washington county. The winds tore off portions of the roof from a nursing home in Millbridge...forcing evacuation of the facility. The winds also downed trees and power lines which contributed to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid airmass was firmly in place across New England with highs well into the 80s across the interior areas away from the coast. A surface cold front swung through the state during the afternoon triggering thunderstorms across interior areas.  A few of these storms became severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped ping pong size in Vienna Maine.

An unseasonably hot and humid airmass was firmly in place across New England with highs well into the 80s across the interior mountains.  A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front triggered a long line of severe thunderstorms that had a history of causing wind damage across Quebec province.  This line of storms reached the Maine border during the evening on May 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective outflow damaging winds caused by a severe thunderstorms downed multiple trees and wires on on State Route 27 in Coburn Gore Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked north across New England and the Saint Lawrence River Valley during the 19th drawing an occluded front across the region. Abundant moisture was drawn north in advance of the front which supported heavy rains across portions of Downeast Maine. Total rainfall across Washington county generally ranged from 2.50 to 3.50 inches...with local totals up to around 4.00 inches. Across Hancock county...totals generally ranged from around 1.50 inches to 3.00 inches with locally greater totals. Minor urban and small stream flooding occurred across portions of Washington county with water covering some roads along with overflowing culverts. Water was reported on portions of Route 1A in Machias and Route 191 in Lubec. Standing water was reported in poor drainage areas along with elevated stream levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranging from 2.50 to 3.50 inches...with local totals up to around 4.00 inches...led to minor urban and small stream flooding. Water was reported on portions of Route 1A in Machias and Route 191 in Lubec. Standing water occurred in poor drainage areas along with elevated stream levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked north across New England and the Saint Lawrence River Valley during the 19th drawing an occluded front across the region. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front. Sustained winds along the Downeast coast generally ranged from 20 to 30 mph...with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. A peak wind gust of 58 mph was reported at Sedgwick Ridge in coastal Hancock county. The strong winds brought down trees and power lines in some areas leading to power outages. Wind gusts across inland areas generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph. However...where the low level jet interacted with higher terrain locally stronger wind gusts also occurred. Wind gusts of around 55 mph occurred in the vicinity of Lily Bay in the mountainous terrain of central Piscataquis county. In excess of 35 trees were reported toppled along Lily Bay Road between Kokadjo and Greenville. The road was closed due to trees and wires across the road. Heavy precipitation also helped to locally mix the strong winds from aloft to the surface producing 40 to 50 mph wind gusts which toppled trees.|High seas...long period swell...onshore winds and high astronomical tides combined to produce coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. Coastal flooding occurred with the early afternoon high tide during the 19th. Seawall Road...near Seawall Pond...in Hancock county was closed due to water and rocks over the road. Several reports of water and sand covering roads were also received from the Roque Bluffs area in Washington county. Conditions improved with the falling tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seawall Road was closed...in the vicinity of Seawall Pond...around the time of the early afternoon high tide on the 19th. The road was closed due to water and rocks covering the road. The road was re-opened with the falling tide once debris was cleared from the road.

In excess of 35 trees were reported toppled along Lily Bay Road between Kokadjo and Greenville. The road was closed due to trees and wires across the road. Heavy precipitation also helped to locally mix the strong winds from aloft to the surface producing 40 to 50 mph wind gusts which toppled trees.|High seas...long period swell...onshore winds and high astronomical tides combined to produce coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. Coastal flooding occurred with the early afternoon high tide during the 19th. Seawall Road...near Seawall Pond...in Hancock county was closed due to water and rocks over the road. Several reports of water and sand covering roads were also received from the Roque Bluffs area in Washington county. Conditions improved with the falling tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet intersecting higher terrain along with winds being mixed down from aloft led to localized high winds and tree damage. In excess of 35 trees were toppled along the Lily Bay Road between Kokadjo and Greenville by wind gusts approaching 60 mph. The road was closed due to fallen trees and wires covering the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rising water levels on the Aroostook River due to rain and snowmelt led to minor flooding and the closure of a portion of the Russell Road...at Fort Fairfield...during the evening of the 11th. The road remained closed overnight. The road was re-opened again during the morning of the 12th with falling river levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated water levels on the Aroostook River due to rain and snowmelt led to minor flooding and closure of a portion of Russell Road in Fort Fairfield from the evening of the 11th into the morning of the 12th. The road was re-opened with falling river levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Saint John River...in the vicinity of Dickey...during the evening of the 12th. Rising river levels led to flooding and the closure of a portion of Route 161...near the Allagash and Saint Francis town line. The road closure was brief. The waters also reached the first floor of one camp...with water also in the basement of another house. The ice jam released and moved around 90 minutes later allowing river levels to fall and allowing the road to re-open once ice was cleared from the road. This evolved into an ice flow which grew to around 9 miles in length. The ice flow occasionally jammed while moving downriver through the morning of the 16th. However...flooding with these additional ice jams was generally minor mostly impacting low lying agricultural flood plain areas. Locations which experienced this minor lowland flooding extended from Allagash...to Fort Kent...to Madawaska...to Van Buren and Hamlin. The ice flow cleared the region during the morning of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Saint John River during the evening of the 12th. Rising water levels in the vicinity of the jam led to flooding and closure along a portion of Route 161 near the Allagash and Saint Francis town line. The road closure was brief...only around 90 minutes...before the ice jam released allowing water levels to recede. The road was re-opened once river levels fell and ice was cleared from the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid airmass was firmly in place across New England with highs well into the 80s across the interior areas away from the coast. A surface cold front swung through the state during the afternoon triggering thunderstorms across interior areas.  A few of these storms became severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped golf ball size hail in Jay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northeast across the Gulf of Maine during the 13th...exiting across the Maritimes that night into the early morning hours of the 14th. Colder air drawn south around the exiting low allowed rain to transition to snow across northern Maine during the evening of the 13th. Snow then persisted into the early morning hours of the 14th. Snow accumulations generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches. However...localized accumulations of 6 to 7 inches occurred across the higher terrain of extreme northern Maine including the Saint John Valley. This was the first measurable snow of the season for many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 90 to the lower 90s...with dewpoints in the upper 60s to around 70...occurred across portions of interior Downeast Maine during the 4th. The combination of temperatures and dewpoints produced apparent temperatures in the mid to upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 90 to the lower 90s...with dewpoints from the upper 60s to around 70...combined to produce apparent temperatures in the mid to upper 90s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures ranged from around 90 to the lower 90s across portions of east-central Maine...to the lower 90s Downeast. Dewpoint temperatures ranged from around 70 to the lower 70s across the region. The combination of temperatures and dewpoints produced apparent temperatures in the mid to upper 90s across east-central areas...with readings in the upper 90s to around 105 Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 90 to the lower 90s....with dewpoints of around 70 to the lower 70s...combined to produce apparent temperatures ranging from the mid 90s to around 100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary remained across the Saint John Valley from the 27th into the 28th. The front served as a focus for heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms through the overnight hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. Along the Saint John Valley of northeast Aroostook county...rainfall totals from the overnight hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th generally ranged from 3.00 to 4.50 inches with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. In some areas...2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain fell in less than 4 hours during the early morning hours of the 28th. The heavy rains led to localized flash flooding which persisted into the late morning of the 28th. Several roads were closed due to rapidly flowing water including Little Madawaska Lake Road...Saint Marys Brook Road and West Road. The cemetary was flooded in the town of Van Buren. A beaver dam collapsed...releasing water which had impounded behind the dam. The surge of water from the release overwhelmed a culvert which led to a washout and total collapse of a portion of Route 1 near Cyr Plantation. The road was closed for nearly a month while repairs were completed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains occurred from the overnight hours of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. Along the Saint John Valley of northeast Aroostook county...rainfall totals generally ranged from 3.00 to 4.50 inches with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. In some areas...2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain fell in less than 4 hours during the early morning hours of the 28th. The heavy rains led to localized flash flooding. Several roads were closed due to flash flooding. A beaver dam collapse led to a surge of water which overwhelmed a culvert...washing out a portion of Route 1. The heavy rains also damaged several dirt roads and gravel driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening primary low approached from the Great Lakes during the 16th. Energy was then transferred to a rapidly intensifying secondary low. The secondary low tracked along the Maine coast during the 16th...exiting across the Maritimes that night. Precipitation expanded across the region through the morning of the 16th. Precipitation was in the form of snow across northern areas...with a wintry mix across central areas and the interior Downeast region. The snow was heavy at times across northern areas during the afternoon with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Snow ended through the evening. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 9 inches across much of northern Maine...with localized totals up to around 12 inches across portions of the Saint John Valley. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon into the evening. Snow accumulations across central areas and the interior Downeast region generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches along with light sleet and freezing rain accumulations. This was the first measurable snow of the season for much of the central and interior Downeast regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 13 inches. Verification is based on new criteria established this season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northwest of Maine drew a strong occluded front across the region during the 30th. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front through the afternoon and evening. The strongest winds occurred along the Downeast coast where southerly winds were generally sustained at 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. However...localized wind gusts up to around 70 mph also occurred. A 69 mph wind gust was reported near Eastport with a 71 mph gust near Trescott...both located in coastal Washington county. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph also occurred across portions of the interior Downeast region. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled. Falling trees and branches brought down power lines leading to power outages for thousands of customers. The greatest impacts occurred across coastal Downeast areas were some roads were also blocked due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to around 70 mph. Most wind gusts ranged from 50 to 60 mph. However...a 71 mph gust was measured at Trescott with a 69 mph gust at Eastport. The winds toppled and snapped trees which fell on power lines leading to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 12th a frontal boundary was stalled over the higher terrain of northern New England. A wave of low pressure developed over the interior of the Mid Atlantic and warm air advection brought light precipitation to the region. With temperatures above freezing most of the precipitation fell in the from of rain, except for the highest elevations where it was below freezing. Low pressure began to deepen as it approached southern New England and that increased the drain of colder air from the north. Eventually precipitation flipped from rain to snow from north to south through the morning. While the bulk of the heavy precipitation was over by the afternoon on the 12th, light snow continued for much of the night with minor additional accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early in the day on the 12th precipitation was mainly rain, especially in the valleys. By mid morning precipitation rapidly changed over to snow as low pressure developed off the coast of southern New England and colder air filtered in from the north. Snow became heavy at times through mid afternoon. Light snow continued through most of the overnight north of the Longfellow Mountains. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 inches in the valleys to locally higher than 14 inches in the higher terrain near Moosehead Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick hitting clipper type low crossed the region on the night of March 2nd through the morning of March 3rd.  Light snowfall developed over most the state of Maine with a general 2 to 4 swath of snow due to brief but moderate snowfall rates.  As the surface boundary approached the Mid-coast region, coastal enhancement due to an interaction with the coastal front and increased low level moisture allowed for a narrow band of heavy snow to develop.  Locally higher snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 occurred in this band due to few hours of heavy snowfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the Mid-coast region of Maine just after midnight on March 3rd. Snow started off light before a localized heavier snowband developed towards the early morning hours across interior Lincoln County and Knox Counties.  Moderate to heavy snowfall fell in this band before it dissipated early in the morning. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

With temperatures above freezing most of the precipitation fell in the from of rain, except for the highest elevations where it was below freezing. Low pressure began to deepen as it approached southern New England and that increased the drain of colder air from the north. Eventually precipitation flipped from rain to snow from north to south through the morning. While the bulk of the heavy precipitation was over by the afternoon on the 12th, light snow continued for much of the night with minor additional accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early in the day on the 12th precipitation was mainly rain, especially in the valleys. By mid-morning precipitation rapidly changed over to snow as low pressure developed off the coast of southern New England and colder air filtered in from the north. Snow became heavy at times through mid-afternoon.  Light snow continued through most of the overnight north of the Longfellow Mountains. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches in the valleys to locally higher than 12 inches above 2000ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 12th a frontal boundary was stalled over the higher terrain of northern New England. A wave of low pressure developed over the interior of the Mid Atlantic and warm air advection brought light precipitation to the region. With temperatures above freezing most of the precipitation fell in the from of rain, except for the highest elevations where it was below freezing. Low pressure began to deepen as it approached southern New England and that increased the drain of colder air from the north. Eventually precipitation flipped from rain to snow from north to south through the morning. While the bulk of the heavy precipitation was over by the afternoon on the 12th, light snow continued for much of the night with minor additional accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early in the day on the 12th precipitation was mainly rain, especially in the valleys. By mid-morning precipitation rapidly changed over to snow as low pressure developed off the coast of southern New England and colder air filtered in from the north. Snow became heavy at times through mi-afternoon. Light snow continued through most of the overnight north of the Longfellow Mountains.  Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches in the valleys to locally higher than 12 inches above 2000ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the region from the 19th into the 20th. Snow began during the morning of the 19th then transitioned to a wintry mix which persisted into the morning of the 20th. Snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches along with up to a half inch of sleet and a quarter inch of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches along with up to a half inch of sleet and up to a quarter inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while moving along the Downeast coast during the morning of the 25th. Warmer air being drawn north over cold low level air dammed at the surface produced a wintry mix across northern Maine through the morning and afternoon. Across northern Aroostook county and northern portions of Somerset and Piscataquis counties snow accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches along with around a tenth of an inch of ice. Across northern portions of Penobscot county snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches along with a tenth to quarter inch of ice. Across southeast Aroostook county snow accumulations were around an inch along with a quarter to half inch of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches along with around a tenth of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Maine to east of Nova Scotia from the overnight hours of the 2nd into the 3rd. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 3rd then persisted through the morning. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 4 to 6 inches...with isolated totals around 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...with an isolated maximum total of 8.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Downeast areas to the Maritimes from the night of the 7th into the 8th. Snow developed during the afternoon of 7th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 8th. Storm total snow accumulations across northwest Aroostook county ranged from 5 to 8 inches. Across northeast Aroostook county...snow accumulations ranged from around 5 inches north to around an inch south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across Downeast areas to the Maritimes from the night of the 7th into the 8th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches...from south to north...across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 90 to the lower 90s...with dewpoints in the upper 60s to around 70...occurred across portions of interior Downeast Maine during the 4th. The combination of temperatures and dewpoints produced apparent temperatures in the mid to upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 90 to the lower 90s...with dewpoints from the upper 60s to around 70...combined to produce apparent temperatures in the mid to upper 90s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick hitting clipper type low crossed the region on the night of March 2nd through the morning of March 3rd.  Light snowfall developed over most the state of Maine with a general 2 to 4 swath of snow due to brief but moderate snowfall rates.  As the surface boundary approached the Mid-coast region, coastal enhancement due to an interaction with the coastal front and increased low level moisture allowed for a narrow band of heavy snow to develop.  Locally higher snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 occurred in this band due to few hours of heavy snowfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the Mid-coast region of Maine just after midnight on March 3rd. Snow started off light before a localized heavier snowband developed towards the early morning hours across interior Lincoln County and Knox Counties.  Moderate to heavy snowfall fell in this band before it dissipated early in the morning. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northeast across Maine during the 12th...exiting across the Maritimes late. Snow developed during the morning of the 12th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 13th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the evening of the 12th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches across much of northern Maine...with 5 to 8 inches across northeast areas. Winds increased in the wake of the low...with gusts of 30 to 40 mph. The gusty winds produced extensive blowing and drifting snow across portions of Aroostook county through the morning and much of the afternoon of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph developed in the wake of the storm which persisted into the afternoon of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and unstable airmass was in place across the region on August 4th with heat index values reaching into the mid-90s during the afternoon hours. A weak mid-level trough provided the region with enough instability for thunderstorms to develop across the Connecticut River Valley during the late afternoon. The storms moved east through the early evening bring strong damaging wind gusts to Oxford County before the storms weakened and moved east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective outflow winds associated with a severe thunderstorm downed a tree and wires on Fern Dr. in Lovell, ME.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a fine line of convection quickly moved through the state on the evening of November 30th. The line was strongest across southwest Maine in Cumberland County where numerous trees and wires were blown down as the storms quickly moved from west to east. A few reports of trees down were also reported across parts of the state but the damage was more concentrated across Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed a large tree at the intersection of Larrabee and Riverside in Westbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of strong cold front and surface low over New York state interacted with a hot and humid airmass on the afternoon of August 26th. A line of thunderstorms developed across Vermont and quickly moved across New Hampshire from west to east during the early to mid afternoon hours. The line of storms reached York and Oxford County by mid-afternoon.  The storms were weakening before a storm re-strengthened over Portland and the Marine waters causing significant damage on Peaks Island waterfront area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty outflow winds associated with a severe thunderstorm downed two 20 diameter trees onto a road in Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 11th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. An isolated severe thunderstorrm produced damaging winds in Madawaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled along Dufour Road by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions developed across Maine and New Hampshire in August 2022. According to the August 2nd, 2022 release of the United States Drought Monitor, 76% of the National Weather Service Weather (NWS) Forecast Office Gray Maine (WFO GYX) County Warning Area (CWA) experienced drought conditions. Roughly 76% of the area was under moderate drought conditions. Approximately 18% of the area was in severe drought focused along coastal Maine. By the August 30th drought monitor 38% of the area was experiencing drought. Roughly 15 percent was in severe drought, and 1 percent was experiencing extreme drought along the shared border with Massachusetts.||The drought was the result of a combination of factors. For portions of northern New Hampshire and western Maine, long term drought conditions defined by reduced groundwater and lake levels carried over from 2021. For most areas, winter failed to deliver the expected amount of snowfall with snowpacks well below normal by spring. Most areas received near normal precipitation in the course of the winter season, though in central and southern counties it frequently fell as rain due to warm temperatures. Overall, the seasonal snowpack was 1 to 3 feet below normal.  If one combines the snowfall deficits from the winter 20-21 and 21-22, the departures are 4 to 7 feet below normal. ||The spring thaw arrived approximately 2 to 4 weeks early, resulting in an earlier than normal discharge along area waterways. The rest of spring lacked the typical rain frequency resulting in below average precipitation for most are as in April and May. Rainfall deficits began in the spring across central and southern counties, however the drying of surface water accelerated in mid June when temperatures began to warm and water demands grew. Most of southern Maine and New Hampshire received one to two inches below normal rainfall in June. In July, below normal rainfall, from 50% to 75% of normal, were common across southern Maine though spotty storm activity led to wide distributions in these areas. Temperatures meanwhile soared, with southern areas 4-5��F above normal. Subsequently, evaporation losses exceeded precipitation amounts resulting in a net loss in surface water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the August 2nd drought monitor, Cumberland County was 56% in severe drought and 44% moderate drought. By the end of the month, 33% of the county was in severe drought, 26% in moderate drought, and 41% abnormally dry. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall, notably on the 22nd where areas received up to 4 inches. The highest accumulated rainfall for the month fell at the Portland Jetport with 5.63 inches for the month. ||The state of Maine activated its Drought Task Force in July 2022. Several towns instituted voluntary water restrictions. Hydro operators balanced needs of lake levels, hydroelectric power generation needs, and the discharges to maintain stream flow needs downstream. Most of the reported water quantity issues were from southern and coastal areas. Farmers without irrigation experienced stressed vegetation from a lack of rain and above normal temperatures in July. First-cut hay crop was near normal, but subsequent hay growth was slowed. Most streamflow stations in central and southern rivers with 30+ years of record were between the 10th-25th percentiles compared to normal.

In July, below normal rainfall, from 50% to 75% of normal, were common across southern Maine though spotty storm activity led to wide distributions in these areas. Temperatures meanwhile soared, with southern areas 4-5��F above normal. Subsequently, evaporation losses exceeded precipitation amounts resulting in a net loss in surface water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the August 2nd drought monitor, Sagadahoc County was 100% in severe drought. By the end of the month, 59% of the county was in severe drought and 41% in moderate drought. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall, however most of the county observed below average amounts under 3 inches.||The state of Maine activated its Drought Task Force in July 2022. Several towns instituted voluntary water restrictions. Hydro operators balanced needs of lake levels, hydroelectric power generation needs, and the discharges to maintain stream flow needs downstream. Most of the reported water quantity issues were from southern and coastal areas. Farmers without irrigation experienced stressed vegetation from a lack of rain and above normal temperatures in July. First-cut hay crop was near normal, but subsequent hay growth was slowed. Most streamflow stations in central and southern rivers with 30+ years of record were between the 10th-25th percentiles compared to normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with moisture laden air moved into the area with dewpoints in the mid 60s to lower 70s. An upper level low slowly moved east through southern New England with a surface low and associated cold front. This supported rounds of convection across the region during the afternoon into the early evening.  A sea breeze front stalled and converged with the warm frontal boundary resulting in a nearly stationary storm with heavy rainfall across southern Maine that produced reports of 4 to 11 inches in York County.  Due to antecedent drought conditions, impacts were limited compared to how much rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall was reported across York County. A local COOP observer in Hollis measured 4.19 from the 22-23rd. The torrential rainfall caused stream levels to rise on the Middle Branch of the Mousam River in Waterboro. The river washed out a 3 ft diameter culvert and large portion of Scooter Way road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked to the north of the region across Quebec province, with a trailing cold front pushing westward across New England during the afternoon hours of July 12th. A hot and unstable airmass was in place across southwest Maine, with above normal temperatures. A pre-frontal trough axis ahead of the surface boundary was the focus area of thunderstorm development. A broken line of Severe Thunderstorms developed in New Hampshire and moved eastward towards Maine.  Most storms began to weaken on the line but a storm traveling through the Lakes Region remained severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow wind gusts downed multiple trees down on Rt. 114 in Sebago, Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 24th across northern Aroostook county within the warm humid air in advance of an approaching cold front. A severe supercell thunderstorm developed across northwest Aroostook county during the late afternoon and tracked northeast across northern Aroostook county through the early evening. The thunderstorm produced damaging winds and large hail at various times along its path. The maximum hail size reported was around 2.50 inches...with wind gusts estimated at up to 90 mph at times. The storm tracked through wooded areas along much of its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with moisture laden air lifted into the area during the day, and an approaching shortwave trough supported developing thunderstorm activity. With the atmospheric moisture content at a daily maximum based on upper air sounding records, torrential rainfall with rainfall rates of 3 inches an hour or greater were anticipated. However, the storms were discrete or small in size and progressed at speeds of 30 mph, limiting coverage and exposure times. Therefore, most locations that received heavy rainfall were able to do so without impacts. The one exception was when a storm cluster moved over the urban areas of Auburn and Lewiston Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain up to 3.5 caused numerous road closures in Sabbatus and some minor road damage to include Elm Street, Jordan Bridge Rd, and Lisbon Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northwest of Maine drew a strong occluded front across the region during the 30th. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front through the afternoon and evening. The strongest winds occurred along the Downeast coast where southerly winds were generally sustained at 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. However...localized wind gusts up to around 70 mph also occurred. A 69 mph wind gust was reported near Eastport with a 71 mph gust near Trescott...both located in coastal Washington county. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph also occurred across portions of the interior Downeast region. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled. Falling trees and branches brought down power lines leading to power outages for thousands of customers. The greatest impacts occurred across coastal Downeast areas were some roads were also blocked due to fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. A 60 mph gust was measured at Bar Harbor...with a 59 mph gust at Castine. The winds toppled and snapped trees which fell on power lines leading to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the northeastern United States ahead of an approaching cold front with these ingredients resulting in numerous showers and thunderstorms across New Hampshire and western Maine.  A severe thunderstorm|with a history of producing large hail and damaging winds moved from central New Hampshire eastward into western Maine and caused significant damage, including concentrated wind damage along the western shore of Sebago Lake due to a microburst. The storm was also accompanied by ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow wind gusts downed a tree down with debris in the road in North Limington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved eastward from the Great Lakes states and into New England on the 25th. The front was preceded by plenty of heat and instability to foster a few strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down on road that caused a car accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on December 22nd a powerful storm was developing over the Ohio River Valley. On December 23rd the center of the storm was deepening rapidly (974mb) as it lifted through the eastern Great Lakes. While the center tracked into southern Ontario, high pressure retreated into the Canadian Maritimes creating a strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong southerly winds developed as a result, with speeds in excess of 80 knots at around 2,000 feet. Several rounds of strong to damaging wind gusts occurred, which brought widespread power outages to the region with upwards to 240,000 customers without power. ||Precipitation moved into southern Maine during the late evening hours of December 22, and moved across the remainder of the state into the early morning of December 23. Snow quickly changed over to rain on the night of December 22nd, with a changeover later in the mountains towards daybreak on December 23rd.  The precipitation continued through the evening hours of December 23, then gradually ended from south to north early morning, December 24. Overland flooding was prevalent for areas where rainfall exceeded 2 inches. The impacts varied from standing water in fields to complete road washouts. The runoff exceeded capacity for most feeder creeks and rivers.||The rapidly deepening area of low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with very high astronomical tides to produce the fourth highest storm tide ever in Portland. In addition, storm to hurricane force east to southeasterly winds occurred along the coast with nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet coinciding during peak storm surge.  Significant to major coastal damage occurred during the morning high tide cycle across the beaches and Mid-Coast of Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of the Midcoast experienced two periods of strong to damaging winds. One during the morning hours of the 23rd generally had wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range. A second during the afternoon and evening featured wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph. Numerous trees and wires were reported down in the area, and power outages approached 30 percent of customers across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the September 1st, 2022 release of the United States Drought Monitor, |84% of the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office Gray Maine County Warning Area (CWA) experienced drought conditions. Roughly 38% of the area was under moderate drought conditions. Approximately 15% of the area was in severe drought, focusing along coastal New Hampshire and Maine. Around 1% of the area experienced extreme drought along the Massachusetts and New Hampshire state line. Rainfall in August reduced drought severity and coverage in Maine with little impact in New Hampshire.  By the end of September rainfall was above normal for the entire region, doubling the averages in some locations. The rainfall brought welcome relief to drought-stricken areas of Maine and New Hampshire and supported a one to two category improvement to the drought designations since the beginning of the month. Apart from a very small geographic area along the New Hampshire and Massachusetts Stateline, the severe drought conditions were downgraded. According to the September 27th, 2022, release of the United States Drought Monitor, 37% of the area experienced abnormally dry conditions. Roughly 15% of the area was under moderate drought conditions, and another 0.35% of the area was in severe drought. Streamflow's were variable during the month, but overall streamflow's were near to above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 30th drought monitor, Cumberland County was 33% in severe drought, 26% moderate drought, and 41% abnormally dry. By the end of the month, 14% of the county was in moderate drought and 36% abnormally dry. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall, with most of the county receiving between 3 to 6 inches. The highest accumulated rainfall for the month fell at Bridgton with 9.16 inches for the month, with 2.21 inches falling on the 5th. A USDA Disaster Designation for drought was applied to Cumberland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 16th low pressure develop near Chesapeake Bay and slowly moved northeastward along the coast to a position near Nantucket late in the day. Eventually low pressure moved into the Gulf of Maine on December 17th and continued towards Nova Scotia. Despite a climatologically favorable track for significant snowfall, the antecedent near surface air mass was marginal for snow south of the mountains. Temperatures aloft were cool and supportive of snow, but rain mixed in south of the foothills.  Due to the heavy wet snow, significant power outages occurred across interior Maine with 80,000 outages reported.  Oxford, Franklin and Somerset Counties were impacted the hardest.  There was a significant upslope and elevation component to snowfall totals when precipitation finally came to an end on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation began on the morning on the 16th. Steady moderate to occasionally heavy snow started relatively quickly after the onset, especially at higher elevations. Southeast flow upsloping into the western Maine Mountains produced significantly more snowfall along the east facing slopes. Precipitation tapered off midday on the 17th. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches near Sebago Lake to 18 inches near Pleasant Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the September 1st, 2022 release of the United States Drought Monitor, |84% of the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office Gray Maine County Warning Area (CWA) experienced drought conditions. Roughly 38% of the area was under moderate drought conditions. Approximately 15% of the area was in severe drought, focusing along coastal New Hampshire and Maine. Around 1% of the area experienced extreme drought along the Massachusetts and New Hampshire state line. Rainfall in August reduced drought severity and coverage in Maine with little impact in New Hampshire.  By the end of September rainfall was above normal for the entire region, doubling the averages in some locations. The rainfall brought welcome relief to drought-stricken areas of Maine and New Hampshire and supported a one to two category improvement to the drought designations since the beginning of the month. Apart from a very small geographic area along the New Hampshire and Massachusetts Stateline, the severe drought conditions were downgraded. According to the September 27th, 2022, release of the United States Drought Monitor, 37% of the area experienced abnormally dry conditions. Roughly 15% of the area was under moderate drought conditions, and another 0.35% of the area was in severe drought. Streamflow's were variable during the month, but overall streamflow's were near to above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 30th drought monitor, Cumberland County was 33% in severe drought, 26% moderate drought, and 41% abnormally dry. By the end of the month, 14% of the county was in moderate drought and 36% abnormally dry. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall, with most of the county receiving between 3 to 6 inches. The highest accumulated rainfall for the month fell at Bridgton with 9.16 inches for the month, with 2.21 inches falling on the 5th. A USDA Disaster Designation for drought was applied to Cumberland County.

The runoff exceeded capacity for most feeder creeks and rivers.||The rapidly deepening area of low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with very high astronomical tides to produce the fourth highest storm tide ever in Portland. In addition, storm to hurricane force east to southeasterly winds occurred along the coast with nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet coinciding during peak storm surge. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning of December 23rd had perfect alignment of a very high astronomical tide, peak storm surge of 2+ feet and large battering nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet all coinciding at high tide.  The astronomical tide in Portland was 11.3 feet MLLW at 10:16 am, already close to the established 12 foot MLLW minor flood level. The intense onshore winds produced a storm surge of 2.41 feet in Portland Harbor.  The resulting storm tide topped out at 13.72 feet MLLW, it���s fourth highest water level in modern history for the Forest City (records 1912-present). The tide remained above flood stage for 195 minutes (945 am - 100 pm). A temporary new tide gauge installed by the Gulf of Maine Research Institute (GMRI) in Bath reported a peak water level of 11.7 feet MLLW at high tide. ||Boothbay Harbor reported flooding of the waterfront piers and footbridges. Some water reached into the base of buildings.  In addition, dock and pier damage was reported due to high water levels and wave action that resulted in structural damage in and around the Boothbay Area.  Pemaquid Beach had up to 20 feet of land erode into the ocean due to wave action.  Minor flooding was reported in Damariscotta where the town parking lots were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient between Post-Tropical Storm Fiona across the Maritimes and high pressure across the Great Lakes produced high winds across northern Aroostook county from the early mornnig hours into the afternoon of the 24th. Sustained northwest winds generally ranged from 20 to 30 mph.  Wind gusts across northern Aroostook county generally ranged from 35 to 45 mph with some gusts in excess of 50 mph. The winds toppled trees and snapped branches at scattered locations across northern Aroostook county. Some of the falling trees brought down power lines contributing to power outages. Power outages peaked at around 5000 customers during the early afternoon of the 24th...mostly across Aroostook county. Wind gusts across the remainder of the region generally ranged from 35 to 45 mph with only isolated reports of downed trees and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with gusts generally 35 to 45 mph. However...some gusts were in excess of 50 mph. The winds occurred from the early morning hours of the 24th into the afternoon. The winds toppled trees and snapped branches at scattered locations...with the most significant impacts across mostly eastern portions of the Saint John Valley including Fort Kent and Madawaska. The falling trees and snapped branches brought down power lines contributing to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the September 1st, 2022 release of the United States Drought Monitor, |84% of the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office Gray Maine County Warning Area (CWA) experienced drought conditions. Roughly 38% of the area was under moderate drought conditions. Approximately 15% of the area was in severe drought, focusing along coastal New Hampshire and Maine. Around 1% of the area experienced extreme drought along the Massachusetts and New Hampshire state line. Rainfall in August reduced drought severity and coverage in Maine with little impact in New Hampshire.  By the end of September rainfall was above normal for the entire region, doubling the averages in some locations. The rainfall brought welcome relief to drought-stricken areas of Maine and New Hampshire and supported a one to two category improvement to the drought designations since the beginning of the month. Apart from a very small geographic area along the New Hampshire and Massachusetts Stateline, the severe drought conditions were downgraded. According to the September 27th, 2022, release of the United States Drought Monitor, 37% of the area experienced abnormally dry conditions. Roughly 15% of the area was under moderate drought conditions, and another 0.35% of the area was in severe drought. Streamflow's were variable during the month, but overall streamflow's were near to above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 30th drought monitor, Waldo County was 6% in severe drought and 88% moderate drought. By the end of the month, 10% of the county was in moderate drought and 89% abnormally dry. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall with most areas receiving between 3 and 5 inches of rain. A USDA Disaster Designation for drought was applied to Waldo County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on December 22nd a powerful storm was developing over the Ohio River Valley. On December 23rd the center of the storm was deepening rapidly (974mb) as it lifted through the eastern Great Lakes. While the center tracked into southern Ontario, high pressure retreated into the Canadian Maritimes creating a strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong southerly winds developed as a result, with speeds in excess of 80 knots at around 2,000 feet. Several rounds of strong to damaging wind gusts occurred, which brought widespread power outages to the region with upwards to 240,000 customers without power. ||Precipitation moved into southern Maine during the late evening hours of December 22, and moved across the remainder of the state into the early morning of December 23. Snow quickly changed over to rain on the night of December 22nd, with a changeover later in the mountains towards daybreak on December 23rd.  The precipitation continued through the evening hours of December 23, then gradually ended from south to north early morning, December 24. Overland flooding was prevalent for areas where rainfall exceeded 2 inches. The impacts varied from standing water in fields to complete road washouts. The runoff exceeded capacity for most feeder creeks and rivers.||The rapidly deepening area of low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with very high astronomical tides to produce the fourth highest storm tide ever in Portland. In addition, storm to hurricane force east to southeasterly winds occurred along the coast with nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet coinciding during peak storm surge.  Significant to major coastal damage occurred during the morning high tide cycle across the beaches and Mid-Coast of Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of the Maine Lakes Region experienced two periods of strong to damaging winds on the 23rd. One during the morning hours generally had wind gusts in the 40 to 55 mph range. A second during the afternoon and evening featured wind gusts around 60 mph, including a 59 mph gust at the Gray/Portland National Weather Service office. Numerous trees and wires were reported down in the area, and power outages approached 30 percent of customers across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice flows from Gorham were transported downstream to Rumford on December 23rd when rain and snowmelt raised river levels. The ice jammed at the Rumford dam, causing fast rising water during the early morning hours of December 24. All three roads into Rumford from the west were flooded. Route 232 and Route 2 were severely flooded with water 1 to 5 feet deep. Flood waters entered around a dozen properties, including the first floor of at least 5 structures. Multiple cold water rescues were conducted for motorists surprised by the flooded roads at night. No fatalities or injuries were reported.
