EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning of December 23rd had perfect alignment of a very high astronomical tide, peak storm surge of 2+ feet and large battering nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet all coinciding at high tide.  The astronomical tide in Portland was 11.3 feet MLLW at 10:16 am, already close to the established 12 foot MLLW minor flood level. The intense onshore winds produced a storm surge of 2.41 feet in Portland Harbor.  The resulting storm tide topped out at 13.72 feet MLLW, it���s fourth highest water level in modern history for the Forest City (records 1912-present). The tide remained above flood stage for 195 minutes (945 am - 100 pm). A temporary new tide gauge installed by the Gulf of Maine Research Institute (GMRI) in Portland reached 13.78 feet MLLW.  Across the southern tip of Maine at the relatively new Seavey Island gauge, the tide reached 12.41 feet MLLW at 1054 am with a storm surge of 2.50 feet.||Significant to major coastal flooding occurred across most vulnerable locations in York County.  No injuries were reported but there were documented water rescues at Granite Point in Biddeford and in Wells on Mile Road as well as Webhannet Drive due to stranded motorists in flood waters. A documented water rescue also occurred in Old Orchard Beach due to residents trapped in their home. ||Flooded roads were widespread along coastal communities.  In Wells, Mile Stretch Rd. and Ocean Avenue was inundated with numerous homes being cut-off as waves crashed through properties bringing seafoam and debris. Roads in the Ocean Park neighborhood of Old Orchard Beach were closed due to 3 feet of inundation that flooded homes and vehicles. A bridge on New Salt Road was damaged by the high water and tidal currents.  Additional inundated roads included Blaine, Sandpiper, Weymouth, Seaside, Randall, Temple, and Colby Avenue causing significant damage to area homes and cottages. The peak water level in Ocean Park did not arrive until a full 90 minutes after high tide due to tidal interactions with a nearby marsh in the Goosefare Brook estuary.  In Saco, significant splash-over flooded roads and ripped up pavement in the Camp Ellis area of North and Main Avenues.  Biddeford experienced numerous roads being flooded, along with significant damage to private property due to wave action in the Fortunes Rocks area.  Mile Stretch Rd., Granite Point Rd., Fortunes Rock Rd., and Hills Beach Rd. were all inundated with up to 2 feet of moving water. Wave damage occurred in Qgunquit as a hotel in Perkins Cove was damaged and the footbridge beach parking lot was inundated.  Flooding and beach erosion was also reported in York and Kennebunkport.

Across the southern tip of Maine at the relatively new Seavey Island gauge, the tide reached 12.41 feet MLLW at 1054 am with a storm surge of 2.50 feet.||Significant to major coastal flooding occurred across most vulnerable locations in Cumberland County.  Significant flooding occurred along the city wharfs and side streets in Portland with inundation of up to 2 feet that included Amethyst Park, the Portland Pier, the Harbor Fish Market, Union Wharf, Widgery Wharf as well as the Custom House Wharf. Portions of Marginal Way also flooded but inundation levels were slightly less.  Flooding also occurred in the Back Cove area to include the Bayside Trail, Sewage Plant Rd. and parts of Commercial Street.  South Portland had flooding along Willard Beach and Bug Light State Park.  The historic seawall of Fort Preble was damaged due to large battering waves.  The iconic Portland Head Light was damaged as large battering waves crashed into the building which blew out windows and flooded the building in Cape Elizabeth.  Additional wave damage to roads with tons of rock debris on roads that had to plowed in Cape Elizabeth. Further south, Scarborough experienced flooding on Black Point Rd. Haggis Parkway, Payne Road and sections of Route 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on December 22nd a powerful storm was developing over the Ohio River Valley. On December 23rd the center of the storm was deepening rapidly (974mb) as it lifted through the eastern Great Lakes. While the center tracked into southern Ontario, high pressure retreated into the Canadian Maritimes creating a strong pressure gradient across the region. Strong southerly winds developed as a result, with speeds in excess of 80 knots at around 2,000 feet. Several rounds of strong to damaging wind gusts occurred, which brought widespread power outages to the region with upwards to 240,000 customers without power. ||Precipitation moved into southern Maine during the late evening hours of December 22, and moved across the remainder of the state into the early morning of December 23. Snow quickly changed over to rain on the night of December 22nd, with a changeover later in the mountains towards daybreak on December 23rd.  The precipitation continued through the evening hours of December 23, then gradually ended from south to north early morning, December 24. Overland flooding was prevalent for areas where rainfall exceeded 2 inches. The impacts varied from standing water in fields to complete road washouts. The runoff exceeded capacity for most feeder creeks and rivers.||The rapidly deepening area of low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with very high astronomical tides to produce the fourth highest storm tide ever in Portland. In addition, storm to hurricane force east to southeasterly winds occurred along the coast with nearshore waves of 15 to 20 feet coinciding during peak storm surge.  Significant to major coastal damage occurred during the morning high tide cycle across the beaches and Mid-Coast of Maine.

The intense onshore winds produced a storm surge of 2.41 feet in Portland Harbor.  The resulting storm tide topped out at 13.72 feet MLLW, it���s fourth highest water level in modern history for the Forest City (records 1912-present). The tide remained above flood stage for 195 minutes (945 am - 100 pm). A temporary new tide gauge installed by the Gulf of Maine Research Institute (GMRI) in Belfast, reported a peak water level of 14.5 feet MLLW at high tide. ||In Belfast minor flooding was reported, Heritage Park was flooded with up to 1 foot of water.  The city parking lot was also flooded with up to 1 foot of inundation along with debris.  Route 1 in Lincolnville Beach area had water over the road and the parking lot was inundated with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the September 1st, 2022 release of the United States Drought Monitor, |84% of the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office Gray Maine County Warning Area (CWA) experienced drought conditions. Roughly 38% of the area was under moderate drought conditions. Approximately 15% of the area was in severe drought, focusing along coastal New Hampshire and Maine. Around 1% of the area experienced extreme drought along the Massachusetts and New Hampshire state line. Rainfall in August reduced drought severity and coverage in Maine with little impact in New Hampshire.  By the end of September rainfall was above normal for the entire region, doubling the averages in some locations. The rainfall brought welcome relief to drought-stricken areas of Maine and New Hampshire and supported a one to two category improvement to the drought designations since the beginning of the month. Apart from a very small geographic area along the New Hampshire and Massachusetts Stateline, the severe drought conditions were downgraded. According to the September 27th, 2022, release of the United States Drought Monitor, 37% of the area experienced abnormally dry conditions. Roughly 15% of the area was under moderate drought conditions, and another 0.35% of the area was in severe drought. Streamflow's were variable during the month, but overall streamflow's were near to above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On August 30th drought monitor, Androscoggin County was 9% in severe drought, 26% in moderate drought, and 64% abnormally dry. By the end of the month, 3% of the county was in moderate drought, and 41% abnormally dry. The improved designations were the result of scattered heavy rainfall. The rainfall for the month ranged from 3 to 5 inches, with the heaviest rain in Auburn with 6.66 inches. A USDA Disaster Designation for drought was applied to Androscoggin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through New England on the night of October 13th before becoming nearly stationary and sparking coastal cyclone development over the region through Saturday October 15th. This front and associated wave of low pressure produced heavy rainfall and isolated flooding to the area, especially in the favored upslope areas of the Western Maine Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swift River came out of banks midday on the 14th and crested at 9.8 feet, almost three feet above flood stage.  The closure of Route 17 in Byron near the intersection of Byron Village Road resulted in significant impact to access in and out of the region. The river flooded as a result of heavy rainfall measured 4.53 inches in Andover in a nearby watershed, with comparable rainfall having fallen over the Swift basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convective cluster moved into the interior sections of central Maine on the evening of the 17th. Another round of moderate to heavy rain aligned over the Kennebec River from Skowhegan to Gardiner. A recent soaking rain event on October 14th had saturated the ground and reduced storage in area ditches and local reservoirs ahead of the storm events on the 17th and 18th. Also compounding the flooding was leaf debris which blocked drainage and culverts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highway department responded to address backed up drainage across multiple roads in the Augusta, Hallowell, and Manchester area on the morning of the 18th. Numerous local roads were flooded as ditches and storm drains were overwhelmed with 2 to 3 inches of rainfall. The heaviest rainfall was reported in Manchester with 2.97 inches which fell in 2 rounds of heavy rain the night before and again during the early morning hours of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through New England on the night of October 13th before becoming nearly stationary and sparking coastal cyclone development over the region through Saturday October 15th. This front and associated wave of low pressure produced heavy rainfall and isolated flooding to the area, especially in the favored upslope areas of the Western Maine Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ellis River reached flood stage on the evening of the 14th cresting at 17.51 feet in the evening. The covered bridge in Andover was flooded along with the adjacent road. Storm total rainfall  was measured at 4.53 inches in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the region during the morning of the 1st leaving warm humid air across the region. An upper level disturbance then crossed the region during the afternoon in advance of an approaching cold front. Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Canopy tents were collapsed and damaged at a Downtown Festival in Houlton. Multiple trees were also reported down across the town. Wind gusts were estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region during the 2nd. Thunderstorms developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon in the warm humid air in advance of the front. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail along the coast of Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening of the 12th in advance of an approaching cold front. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large softwood trees were snapped in the Sandy Bay area of Moosehead Lake by wind  gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large pine trees were toppled on a camp by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pontoon boat was flipped on Long Lake...with several docks also flipped or thrown. A fishing shack was destroyed...with shingles torn from several roofs. Several trees were uprooted or snapped around the lake. Wind gusts were estimated at up to 70 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening of the 12th in advance of an approaching cold front. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 dozen softwood trees were snapped at the base or uprooted...along with several hardwood trees. A small shed was destroyed...with shingles also torn from the roof of a house. Wind gusts were estimated at up to 70 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 14th. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced damaging downburst winds in a wooded area near Grand Lake Stream in Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of 20 to 30 trees were snapped or uprooted near Grand Lake Stream by wind gusts estimated at up to 80 mph. The trees were generally softwood...with a few hardwood...mostly with diameters in the 8 to 15 inch range. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong summer low pressure drew a cold front across the region during the afternoon of the 19th. A strong upper level disturbance also crossed the region along with the cold front. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon. Several of the thunderstorms reached severe levels...producing damaging winds. The most extensive wind damage occurred across northeast Aroostook county along the Route 1 corridor from Mars Hill...to Bridgewater...to Monticello. Several reports of tree damage occurred in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled around Blaine and Mars Hill by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled or snapped around Bridgewater by wind gusts estimated at up to 70 mph. Extensive tree damage was reported along Corner Road...which remained closed into the early morning of the 20th while trees and debris were removed from the road. Numerous trees were snapped or toppled along the Boundary Line Road which blocked access to the Bridgewater Point Of Entry for a time. A large tree toppled across Route 1 resulted in a road closure until the tree was cleared.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn under construction was collapsed by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. Several trees were also toppled. The time is estimated.

A hot and humid airmass was in place across the northeastern United States ahead of an approaching cold front with these ingredients resulting in numerous showers and thunderstorms across New Hampshire and western Maine.  A severe thunderstorm|with a history of producing large hail and damaging winds moved from central New Hampshire eastward into western Maine and caused significant damage, including concentrated wind damage along the western shore of Sebago Lake due to a microburst. The storm was also accompanied by ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow wind gusts downed 15 trees in Cupsuptic area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front with moisture laden air lifted into the area during the day, and an approaching shortwave trough supported developing thunderstorm activity. With the atmospheric moisture content at a daily maximum based on upper air sounding records, torrential rainfall with rainfall rates of 3 inches an hour or greater were anticipated. However, the storms were discrete or small in size and progressed at speeds of 30 mph, limiting coverage and exposure times. Therefore, most locations that received heavy rainfall were able to do so without impacts. The one exception was when a storm cluster moved over the urban areas of Auburn and Lewiston Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates with 3.5 of water caused flooding inside a recreational building with up to 2 feet of standing water on roads in the Pettingil Park area of Auburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and upper level low interacted with ample low level instability to develop thunderstorms across interior Maine during the afternoon of June 4th.  A few of these storms became severe as large hail associated with the cold air aloft became the dominate severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail the size of quarters in Denmark, Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface and upper level trof...along with an associated cold pool aloft... crossed the region through the morning into the afternoon of the 1st. Steep lapse rates and instability with the cold pool aloft...along with gusty winds from a tight pressure gradient...helped support the development of snow squalls during the afternoon. Localized snow squalls accompanied the upper trof during the afternoon. The snow squalls produced wind gusts up to around 40 mph along with brief heavy snow which reduced visibility below a quarter mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. Localized snow squalls produced wind gusts up to around 40 mph along with brief heavy snow which reduced visibility below a quarter mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed through the Western Maine Mountains by late afternoon on June 13th, with a line of showers and thunderstorms sinking south and east.  The storms became stronger once they reached the Kennebec River Valley where a line formed and one of the storms briefly became severe.  The line weakened once again as it approached the coast where the marine layer held strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Readfield, ME.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed through the Western Maine Mountains by late afternoon on June 13th, with a line of showers and thunderstorms sinking south and east.  The storms became stronger once they reached the Kennebec River Valley where a line formed and one of the storms briefly became severe.  The line weakened once again as it approached the coast where the marine layer held strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow wind gusts downed trees onto wires on Notch Rd. in Canaan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking northeast through Quebec province drew a cold front across the region through the afternoon and evening of the 2nd. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front. South winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts generally up to around 60 mph...along the Downeast coast. A peak wind gust of 66 mph was reported at the top of Cadillac Mountain in Acadia National Park. The winds brought down trees which led to the closure of several roads along the coast. The falling trees and snapped branches also brought down power lines which contributed to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained south winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts generally up to around 60 mph...brought down trees and snapped branches. The falling trees and snapped branches brought down power lines which contributed to power outages. Several roads were blocked and closed due to fallen trees. A peak wind gust of 66 mph was reported at the summit of Cadillac Mountain in Acadia National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system and strong divergent flow aloft combined with a muggy airmass to create an unstable airmass on the afternoon of July 14th across Maine.  Morning cloud cover limited daytime heating, but enough clearing allowed for a line of thunderstorms to develop across the I-95 corridor.  A few strong storms developed along with and isolated severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow wind gusts downed a tree and utility pool which fell onto a parked car in Oakland Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the northeastern United States ahead of an approaching cold front with these ingredients resulting in numerous showers and thunderstorms across New Hampshire and western Maine.  A severe thunderstorm|with a history of producing large hail and damaging winds moved from central New Hampshire eastward into western Maine and caused significant damage, including concentrated wind damage along the western shore of Sebago Lake due to a microburst. The storm was also accompanied by ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm and associated microburst reached the western shore of Sebago Lake just after 4pm.  The damaging winds downed 80 to 100 trees...some uprooted and others snapped.  Most of the trees were softwoods while a few were hardwoods. About fifteen structures were damaged along with ten vehicles.  One fatality occurred when a tree fell on an occupied vehicle.  The most significant damage occurred within a one mile path width and within a quarter mile of the western shore of Sebago Lake. Sporadic damage occurred beyond this.  This storm would continue to produce|additional wind damage as it moved east of Sebago Lake later in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the region during the morning of the 1st leaving warm humid air across the region. An upper level disturbance then crossed the region during the afternoon in advance of an approaching cold front. Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the late afternoon and persisted into the evening. Some of the thunderstorms reached severe levels producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Frenchville ASOS reported a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed along the Penobscot River during the afternoon of the 27th near Howland...which persisted into January 1st. The ice jam led to minor flooding in the vicinity of Merrill Brook Lane and Garden Lane. The flooding impacted a low lying bridge which cut off access to several residents on an island. Rising waters behind the ice jam also eventually impacted Mattamiscontis Road near Gordon Brook. A water rescue was required due to a vehicle being driven into the flood waters and stalling. No injuries occurred. The ice jam eventually reached 2 to 3 miles in length. The ice jam released during the morning of January 1st allowing river levels to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Penobscot River...near Howland...during the afternoon of the 27th and persisted into January 1st. The ice jam produced minor flooding along Merrill Brook Lane and Garden Lane. The flooding also impacted a low lying bridge. A water rescue was required due to a vehicle being driven into the flood waters and stalling. The ice jam released during the morning of January 1st allowing river levels to fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the region during the morning of the 17th leaving warm humid air across the region. A strong upper level disturbance then began to cross the region during the afternoon. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in the vicinity of Easton in northeast Aroostook county.

A warm front crossed the region during the morning of the 17th leaving warm humid air across the region. A strong upper level disturbance then began to cross the region during the afternoon. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in the vicinity of Easton in northeast Aroostook county.

Primary low pressure tracked to the Great Lakes region from the 16th to the 17th...while transferring energy to a developing secondary low along the Mid-Atlantic coast. At the same time...strong blocking high pressure was located near Newfoundland. The blocking high slowed the movement of the intensifying coastal low which reached the Gulf of Maine during the 17th. The low then slowly exited across the Maritimes through the night of the 17th through the 18th. Snow gradually expanded across central and Downeast portions of the region through the evening of the 16th into the early morning hours of the 17th. Drier air with the blocking high delayed the arrival of snow across northern areas until the morning of the 17th. Deformation zone snows with a slow moving upper low then enhanced snowfall across northern and eastern areas through the 17th into the 19th. Snow diminished across the remainder of the Downeast region into the early morning hours of the 18th. The slow moving upper low...along with surface troffing...helped support a prolonged snow event across northern and eastern portions of the region into the morning of the 19th. Snowfall rates during this event reached 1 to 2 inches per hour at times in the heavier snow bands. Warning criteria snow accumulations across central and interior Downeast regions occurred through the morning into the afternoon of the 17th. Across northern areas...warning criteria generally occurred through the evening of the 17th into the morning of the 18th. However...warning criteria accumulations across northwest areas did not occur until the afternoon of the 18th. Storm total snow accumulations across northern and central areas generally ranged from 10 to 18 inches...with up to around 24 inches across the higher terrain of Baxter State Park. Accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 8 to 15 inches. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph led to areas of blowing and drifting snow.  A transition to rain limited accumulations along the Downeast coast to generally 1 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful low pressure rapidly intensified across the Great Lakes from the 22nd into the 23rd...tracking to Quebec province during the 24th. A secondary low developed across western New England during the 23rd from the occluding Great Lakes low. The secondary low then drew an occluded front across the region through the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. This system brought multiple impacts to the region including very strong winds...heavy rain...thunderstorms...coastal flooding and stream flooding.|A very strong low level jet developed in advance of the occluded front. Winds increased through the 23rd. The strongest winds occurred from the afternoon of the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. East to southeast winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts generally 60 to 70 mph. However...localized gusts up to around 80 mph also occurred. An 80 mph gust was measured near Eastport...with a 78 mph gust at Trescott...both in coastal Washington county. A 77 mph wind gust was reported at Frenchville in northern Aroostook county. The winds caused extensive tree damage with trees uprooted and snapped. The falling trees brought down power lines and utility poles leading to extensive power outages. Power outages peaked during the early morning hours of the 24th when around 55000 customers were without power. Roads were also blocked by fallen trees. A line of thunderstorms accompanied the occluded front across western portions of the region which also locally enhanced winds. The gradient winds and thunderstorms led to several hundred trees uprooted or snapped around the Lily Bay area of central Piscataquis county. The winds also tore the roof from a trailer in Caribou and blew a tractor trailer off the road near Fort Fairfield...both in northeast Aroostook county.|Strong onshore winds and large breaking waves led to coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast during the 23rd. The greatest impacts occurred around the time of the late morning high tide. The storm surge at the time of high tide was up to around 2 feet at some locations along with large breaking waves. Several roads and causeways were closed due to overwash...water covering the roads along with deposited debris. Closures included Seawall Road...Moore Road and Hulls Cove Road all in Hancock county. Some roads and causeways were able to re-open after waters subsided and debris cleared. Plows were needed to remove debris from some roads. However...some roads and causeways were damaged or partially washed out leading to more extensive damage. Piers in several harbors were also damaged to varying extents along with beach erosion. Reverse tidal flooding up the Penobscot River led to minor flooding of park land along the Kenduskeag stream during the late evening high tide.|Abundant moisture was also drawn north across the region in advance of the occluded front leading to heavy rain. Warm air was rapidly drawn north. This led to limited wintry precipitation across central and northern areas. Snow accumulations...before the transition to rain...were generally an inch or less across central and northern areas with 1 to 3 inches across extreme northwest areas. Ice accumulations were generally a tenth of an inch or less. The heaviest rains occurred through the afternoon into the evening of the 23rd. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches...with local totals approaching 3.00 inches. Rainfall across northern areas generally ranged from around 0.50 inch to 1.50 inches. The warm temperatures led to melting of the snow pack which released additional water for runoff.  Clogged storm drains helped lead to ponding of water in some areas. Ponding also occurred in some low lying areas. The greatest impacts occurred across portions of central and southern Piscataquis county where several roads and culverts were damaged or washed out and numerous basements were also flooded.|A flash freeze also occurred in the wake of the occluded front during the morning of the 24th with rapidly falling temperatures. Wet roads and standing water rapidly froze leading to slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East to southeast winds were generally sustained at 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 60 to 70 mph. However...a peak wind gust of 77 mph was measured at Frenchville.

The winds also tore the roof from a trailer in Caribou and blew a tractor trailer off the road near Fort Fairfield...both in northeast Aroostook county.|Strong onshore winds and large breaking waves led to coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast during the 23rd. The greatest impacts occurred around the time of the late morning high tide. The storm surge at the time of high tide was up to around 2 feet at some locations along with large breaking waves. Several roads and causeways were closed due to overwash...water covering the roads along with deposited debris. Closures included Seawall Road...Moore Road and Hulls Cove Road all in Hancock county. Some roads and causeways were able to re-open after waters subsided and debris cleared. Plows were needed to remove debris from some roads. However...some roads and causeways were damaged or partially washed out leading to more extensive damage. Piers in several harbors were also damaged to varying extents along with beach erosion. Reverse tidal flooding up the Penobscot River led to minor flooding of park land along the Kenduskeag stream during the late evening high tide.|Abundant moisture was also drawn north across the region in advance of the occluded front leading to heavy rain. Warm air was rapidly drawn north. This led to limited wintry precipitation across central and northern areas. Snow accumulations...before the transition to rain...were generally an inch or less across central and northern areas with 1 to 3 inches across extreme northwest areas. Ice accumulations were generally a tenth of an inch or less. The heaviest rains occurred through the afternoon into the evening of the 23rd. Rainfall totals across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches...with local totals approaching 3.00 inches. Rainfall across northern areas generally ranged from around 0.50 inch to 1.50 inches. The warm temperatures led to melting of the snow pack which released additional water for runoff.  Clogged storm drains helped lead to ponding of water in some areas. Ponding also occurred in some low lying areas. The greatest impacts occurred across portions of central and southern Piscataquis county where several roads and culverts were damaged or washed out and numerous basements were also flooded.|A flash freeze also occurred in the wake of the occluded front during the morning of the 24th with rapidly falling temperatures. Wet roads and standing water rapidly froze leading to slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East to southeast winds were generally sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 65 to 75 mph. However...a peak wind gust of 80 mph was measured near Eastport with a 78 mph gust reported near Trescott.
